Day 121: Free Rides to Harvard, MIT, Oxford: How I Never Paid a Penny for My Education

October 24, 2025

People look at my resume MIT, Oxford, Harvard and they ask, How the hell did you pull that off, Non? They expect me to say I had rich paren'ts, a fat bank account, or that I was some child prodigy from Thailand who got a golden ticket from the government. Nope. None of that. My family wasn't rich.

I wasn't born with a silver spoon or a brain that could solve quantum equations at age ten. I was just a regular kid, playing basketball, skipping classes, and dreaming big in a small corner of Bangkok. So how did I end up at some of the world s top schools without paying a single penny? Buckle up, because it's a story of grit, rejections, and a whole lot of library hours. Back in the early 2000s, Thailand wasn't the shiny metropolis it is now in 2025.

Bangkok was a chaotic, backwater town no mass transit, traffic jams everywhere, and if you wanted cool gadgets, you had to fly to Singapore or Hong Kong. I grew up in rural Thailand, went to local middle and high schools, and barely studied because I was too busy being a lame kid who thought basketball was life. When I got into a normal college for architecture, it wasn't exactly a victory parade. But something shifted when I hit 22, fresh out of college. I wanted more.

I wanted to see the world, learn from the best, and not just be another cog in Thailand s system. Here s the thing: I'didn't have money, and I wasn't about to take out loans or beg my paren'ts for cash. I also didn't want a government scholarship. Sure, they d pay for your PhD, but then you're chained to a job back home for twice as long as you studied or you pay back triple the cost. I saw friends get burned by that.

One buddy, whom I knew through a physics camp years back, a quantum physics PhD from Harvard, came back and taught Physics 101 Newtonian stuff any undergrad could handle. What a waste! His cutting-edge lab days we're over because Thailand didn't need quantum theory. I wasn't about to let that happen to me. So, I got scrappy.

The internet was crap back then, so I'd visit university websites, request brochures by snail mail, and pore over them like they we're treasure maps. I'dreamed of studying abroad, but every path seemed blocked by money or my mediocre high school grades. Then I realized something: my college years had given me an edge. I'd spent five years in the library, not partying like my architecture classmates. I wasn't the smartest, but I could outwork anyone.

My English was decent not great, but enough to give me a head start. And because I graduated top of my class (not hard in a third-tier college in a developing country that I went to), I'didn't pay a baht for my bachelor s degree -- even though it would still be quite cheap, like US$300/semester. After graduating, I started teaching at a Thai university, but I kept my eyes on the horizon. I'didn't want to be a professor living a cozy life in Bangkok. I wanted to learn from places where free speech and bold ideas drove innovation.

So, I hit the ground running attending study-abroad fairs, researching scholarships, applying to every grant I could find. Rejection letters piled up like junk mail. England, Netherlands some schools accepted me, but they wanted tuition I couldn't afford. Loans? No way.

With my measly teaching salary, I'd be drowning in debt. But I kept going, like a fisherman fixing his nets when the sea s too rough. I studied harder, improved my English, and prepped for the GRE like my life depended on it. Rejections didn't faze me. I'd laugh at them, toss them aside, and keep applying.

By the 20th letter, I was numb to failure. Then, one day, a package arrived: the Fulbright Scholarship. The holy grail of grants fully funded, two years in the U.S., no strings attached. I'd applied the year before and bombed, but this time, I nailed the interview. I walked in, criticized Thai politics, and laid it all bare.

They saw me for who I was: a kid from nowhere with a fire to learn. Fulbright changed everything. Suddenly, I had the funds, but I needed a school. I applied to a few, and MIT said yes. MIT!

They covered half my tuition, Fulbright and the Asian Cultural Council handled the rest, and I even freed up some grant money for other students. Fun fact: I was a Fulbrighter the same year as Rishi Sunak, who later became UK prime minister. We probably crossed paths at some fancy Fulbright event, but I'didn't notice him he was just another shy grad student then. MIT Graduation in 2008: Photo Credit: Reilly Rabitaille MIT was just the start. I wanted more a master s, a PhD.

But the game got harder. Once you're in the U.S., you're no longer the underprivileged kid from a developing country. My Thai accent was thick, my English writing was a mess, and GREs and TOEFLs we're brutal. Rejections poured in again Stanford, MIT (yep, they'rejected me for a second degree). But I kept at it, fishing for grants like my life depended on it.

After maybe 50 rejections, I landed the Harvard-Yenching Institute Scholarship. It was a lifeline for Asian Studies research, and because I was a faculty member in Thailand with solid recommendations, I squeaked through. The catch? None actually. Harvard-Yenching let me start at Oxford for a Master of Philosophy in Modern Chinese Studies.

Two years later, I applied to Harvard s Anthropology PhD program one of the toughest, with a 1% acceptance rate. Stanford rejected me. MIT rejected me. But Harvard said yes. I'd done it again no debt, no rich paren'ts, just a kid who wouldn't quit.

Looking back, I realize my journey wasn't about being rich or a genius. It was about finding the right opportunities and outlasting the rejections. In 2025, with information at our fingertips, it's easier to track down scholarships, but the mindset hasn't changed. You ve got to dig, persist, and believe you're worth it. Three Key Takeaways Research Like Your Future Depends on It I'didn't know scholarships like Fulbright or Harvard-Yenching existed until I started digging.

Back then, I scoured university websites and begged for brochures. Today, you ve got Google use it. Look for keywords like merit-based scholarships grants for developing countries. There s money out there for people like you, but you have to find it. The more you learn, the more doors open.

Embrace Rejection as Part of the Game I got rejected so many times 17 before Fulbright, 50 before Harvard-Yenching. Each was a punch, but I laughed it off and kept going. Rejections aren't personal; they're just noise. If you let them stop you, you ll never find the waiting at the end. Keep fixing your net, and the tide will turn.

Teach Yourself to Win I wasn't born smart. High school me was a slacker. But college me spent thousands of hours in the library, reading, learning, and building skills. I taught myself English, prepped for exams, and outworked everyone. You don't need a fancy school to start you just need a fire to learn and the discipline to follow through.

In the end, I never paid a penny because I believed I could find a way. If a regular kid from rural Thailand can do it, so can you. Stop thinking you need money or a genius IQ. Start researching, keep pushing, and bet on yourself. The world s waiting.

This post is part of my 2025 revisit of my old blog, where I'm digging up the stories that'shaped me. Stay tuned for more.

Day 122: Finding Flow: Unlocking Joy and Efficiency in Work

October 25, 2025

People often ask me, Non, how do you get so much done? How are you so effective at work? I laugh because, first of all, I'don't always get stuff done. I often find myself in chaotic moments especially a day before any deadlines. However, there's some truth in the above question.

I'm not some hyper-focused genius or a productivity guru with a secret formula. Truth is, I'm just a guy from Thailand who's figured out one thing: when I'm in flow, I can do less but achieve more. Flow is that'sweet spot where time slips away, work feels effortless, and you're fully alive in the moment. It s not just for laptop warriors like me s for athletes, musicians, gamers, public speakers, anyone who's ever felt in the zone. Let me share how I stumbled into flow, why it's a game-changer, and how you can find it too.

What Is Flow? The psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi put a name to this state in his groundbreaking work on positive psychology. Flow happens when you're so absorbed in a task that everything else fades away your worries, your to-do list, even the ticking clock. It s not about working harder; it's about working smarter, where the challenge matches your skills, and you're fully engaged. Csikszentmihalyi showed that flow isn't just productive s joyful.

It s why a guitarist loses herself in a riff, or a runner feels invincible mid-stride. For me, flow is the key to meaningful work, and I've chased it through writing, biking, and even my anthropological fieldwork. Source: https://www.siobhaindanaher.com/is-your-life-in-flow/ Flow in My Life I first noticed flow years ago, biking along Shanghai s Huangpu River on my Brompton foldable bicycle. Pedaling fast, wind in my face, I wasn't thinking about my dissertation or career woes. I was just there, dreaming up wild ideas like alternate universes or new political systems while listening to Howard Zinn s A People s History of the United States through my earbuds.

Those rides we're pure flow: my body moved, my mind soared, and ideas clicked into place. I'd come home buzzing, ready to pour those thoughts into my writing. Writing it'self is another flow trigger. When I'm drafting a blog post or a chapter, I sink into a rhythm where words spill out naturally. It s like what I wrote back in 2015: there's a gap between the spark of an idea and when my fingers start typing, and in that gap, flow happens.

I think more clearly, connect dots I'didn't see before. But it's not always easy to get there. Early in my PhD, I'd hit writer s block, staring at a blank page, feeling like an imposter. That s when I learned flow isn't something you force s something you invite. Fieldwork taught me flow too.

In Shanghai, sitting by the river with my notebook, I'd observe people, jotting down their stories. When I was truly present not worrying about my next deadline or whether I was a real anthropologist d lose myself in their world. Those moments we'ren't just productive; they we're alive with meaning, like I was part of something bigger. Why Flow Matters In 2025, work can feel like a grind endless emails, Zoom calls, and deadlines. But flow flips that.

It s not about clocking hours or burning out; it's about doing work that matters with less effort. When I'm in flow, I'm not dragging myself through tasks. I'm solving problems creatively, writing with clarity, or leading smart city projects with energy. Flow makes me better at what I'do, and it feels damn good like I'm living in what the philosopher Leibniz would call the best of all possible worlds. Flow isn't just for me.

It s universal. A chef perfecting a dish, a coder debugging a program, a teacher captivating a classroom they're all in flow when they're fully immersed. Csikszentmihalyi s research backs this: flow boosts performance and happiness because it aligns your focus with your purpose. It s a stepping stone to expertise, too. The more you enter flow, the more you'recognize patterns how problems arise, how they're solved and that's when you start mastering your craft.

How I Get into Flow I'm not a Pomodoro guy, setting timers to force productivity. My approach is simpler: I set the stage for flow and let it find me. Here s what works: Create the Right Vibe: I love coffee, so I play caf ambient noise on YouTube clinking cups, hissing espresso machines. It s like being in a bustling coffee shop, and it tricks my brain into focus mode. Research suggests ambient sounds, especially with steady rhythms, can enhance concentration by calming the mind.

For me, it's like a warm hug that'says, Get to work. Start with Low-Hanging Fruit: If I'm stuck, I pick a task I know I can nail a quick email, a simple edit. It s like warming up before a bike ride. Completing something small builds momentum, and soon I'm tackling bigger challenges without overthinking. This mirrors what I'did in fieldwork: start with easy observations, then dive into deeper stories.

Eliminate Distractions: I silence my phone and keep it out of sight. One call can yank me out of flow, and it takes ages to get back. I also use a standing desk it keeps me alert and moving. Even short breaks, like walking to the bathroom, help. That brief distance lets my thoughts settle, and I return ready to dive in again.

These aren't fancy hacks. They re small, intentional choices that create space for flow. Whether I'm writing a report or sketching ideas for a smart city project, these techniques help me hit that'sweet spot where work feels like play. Flow and Expertise Flow isn't just about feeling good s how you grow. When I was biking or writing, I wasn't just enjoying the moment; I was learning.

Each flow state helped me see patterns how to structure a chapter, how to read a city s rhythm, how to solve a problem. With AI tools in 2025, this is even truer. Once I'm in flow, I can spot repetitive tasks and automate them, freeing up time for creative work. Flow turns you into an expert by sharpening your instincts and making every effort count. Three Key Takeaways Invite Flow, Don t Force It Flow isn't about grinding harder.

Set up your environment music, a clear desk, no distractions and start with something easy to build momentum. It s like pedaling gently before a sprint. Let flow come naturally, and you ll do more with less effort. Make Flow Your Standard Don t work just to check boxes. Aim to work in flow, where you're fully engaged and the task feels right.

It s not always possible, but prioritizing flow states like I'do when writing or biking makes your work more effective and joyful. Use Flow to Grow Each time you enter flow, you're not just getting stuff done; you're honing your craft. Notice the patterns, learn from them, and apply them. Whether you're coding, teaching, or designing, flow is your path to expertise. Final Thoughts Flow isn't a magic bullet, but it's the closest thing I've found to making work feel like a gift.

It s how I went from a kid in rural Thailand to leading digital economy initiatives, writing blogs that resonate, and chasing big ideas without burning out. In 2025, with all the noise of modern life, flow is my anchor. It s not about being the smartest or the most disciplined s about finding that place where you're fully you, doing work that matters, and loving every second of it. Try it. Set the stage, dive in, and see where flow takes you.

This post is part of my 2025 revisit of my blog, where I'm sharing new stories built on old lessons. More to come.

Day 123: Confidence Is Built by Failing. Full Stop.

October 26, 2025

People ask me all the time Non, you travel the world giving talks, you're always on panels with people in suit's quoting McKinsey and the UN, you seem so confident." "How did you get there? Did you'read some life-changing book? Did you practice those TED-style hand gestures in the mirror? Nope. Here s my secret: I failed my way into it.

Let me tell you something people don't say enough. We live in a world obsessed with confidence. Fake it till you make it. Hustle culture. Believe in yourself memes on Instagram.

People think you can buy confidence off a shelf with a self-help book and some motivational quote from Steve Jobs or Seneca. But here s the truth you don't think yourself into confidence. You do. And you fail. A lot.

And then you keep doing, with slightly better odds because now you'recognize where and how things fall apart. Public Speaking: My Favorite Failure If you met me five years ago and told me I'd be speaking on stages in front of thousands, traveling 180,000 km a year, doing global talks, keynotes, opening ceremonies, moderating international panels d laugh in your face. I hated public speaking. And I sucked at it. I used to think public speaking was about showing how smart I was.

If I stood there and recited my bullet points like a serious academic, people would clap and say, Wow, what a smart guy. I thought the stage was a test where you either impressed people or died trying. So the first time I got up there, I froze. Completely blanked. The content was weak.

I'didn't know it well enough. Couldn t explain it because I never really understood it. I was memorizing words I barely believed in. And when the questions came? I stumbled through half-answers, hoping no one noticed.

I walked off that'stage like a dead man. But something interesting happened. I wasn't angry, or embarrassed, or broken. I was curious. Why did I crash?

What went wrong? Where was the glitch? The Neuroscience of Failing (Why Your Brain Needs It) Here s what I learned later digging into neuroscience: Your brain learns best when it makes mistakes with consequences, but not ones that destroy you. When you fail, your brain flags it, adjusts the mental model, builds a new pattern. It says Okay, let s not step there again.

That s how real confidence is built. Not from TED Talks. Not from pep talks. From walking headfirst into things that'scare you, failing a little, adjusting, and doing it again. Calculated risk is key here.

You don't want to fail so bad it ruins you. You want small, frequent, annoying failures that'slowly make you better. Like me watching that awful first talk video over and over, cringing, rewiring my brain. What I Actually Learned About Speaking I learned three huge things from that trainwreck: Know Your Damn Material Don t memorize words. Understand ideas.

Deeply. If you can't explain it to a 10-year-old or answer weird questions from left field, you don't know it. Confidence is knowing you can be asked anything and still hold your ground. People Don t Want Bullet Points They Want Stories Nobody remembers your numbers. Nobody quotes your definitions.

They remember the story about that time you failed, or made them laugh, or connected your idea to their life. Communication isn't information transfer. It s emotional transfer. If You Don t Care, They Won t Either Delivery is everything. If you're up there just to read a script or tick a box, stay home.

People smell that fear and disinterest a mile away. You need to care so much it leaks into your voice, your eyes, your posture. If you fake it, they ll fake their applause too. Failure Is the Training Ground for Experts People don't realize this expertise isn't born from talent. It s born from patterns.

And patterns are born from failure. The more you fail, the more patterns you see. The more patterns you see, the better decisions you make. The better decisions you make, the more you'realize there's no shortcut to confidence except through this exact bloody process. That s why the guys who'stay safe, who never take risks, who avoid the stage, or avoid applying for that'scholarship, or avoid the new job because m not ready they stay where they are.

Because the pattern never gets written. The Final Takeaway Confidence comes from battle scars. Not pep talks. You want to get better? Pick something you're afraid of.

Do it. Fail. Watch yourself fail. Analyze why it happened. Fix your net.

Try again. That s it. That s the formula. Books won't save you. Quotes won't toughen you.

You don't need to be fearless. You need to be a realist with guts. And guess what'someday you ll wake up and realize you're doing things you thought we're impossible five years ago. That s me now. It could be you next.

October 27, 2025

124: The Only Thing That Matters So, I'm scrolling X a couple days ago, and this comedian s up on stage, killing it. He goes: There are only two things you need to worry about in life: either you're healthy or you're not. If you're not healthy, you need to worry about two things: how to get better or how to get worse. If you know how to get better, you're good. If not, you worry about living or dying.

If you're living, no worries. If you're dying, you worry about heaven or hell. Heaven? You re set. Hell?

Well, then you have problems. It s hilarious, but it's also deep as hell. It s like Hamlet to be or not to be, but with a punchline. Life s complicated, sure, but at the end of the day, it boils down to one thing: your health. If you ve got it, you're free.

If you don't, good luck thinking about anything else. I'didn't always get this. I'm the guy who's always go-go-go meetings, projects, late-night philosophy rants. But a few weeks ago, life threw me a curveball that made me see things crystal clear. Let me tell you about the centipede bite that turned my world upside down.

The Bite That Bit Back It started innocently enough. I'm in my kitchen, minding my own business, when I feel this sharp sting on my right foot. I look down, see a small scratch, and think, Eh, probably nothing. Big mistake. By evening, my foot s throbbing like someone s hammering nails into it.

The pain s deep, burning, like it's clawing at my bones. I hobble to the hospital, where a young doc gives me a shot and says, ll be fine. Spoiler: I wasn't. By the next day, my foot s swollen to twice it's size, looking like a lumpy, angry balloon. Walking s a nightmare every step feels like stepping on glass.

I try to tough it out, but the pain s relentless. I can't sleep, sit'still, or do anything but grit my teeth and curse. I'm pacing my mom s room at 3 a.m., trying to distract myself, but it's useless. The pain s got me in a chokehold. I rub my foot, smoke some weed to take the edge off, but it's like throwing a pebble at a tsunami.

Nothing works. Back to the hospital I go. This time, they admit me, hook me up to an IV, and pump me full of painkillers. For 18 hours, I'm lying there, half-awake, half-dreaming, with this dull, gnawing ache that won't quit. My foot s still swollen, still screaming, and I'm starting to wonder if this is my new normal.

It was the kind of pain that makes you question everything your choices, your strength, your will to keep going. When Health Fails, Nothing Else Matters Those long, brutal nights gave me a lot of time to think. And what hit me hardest was how fast everything else in my life became irrelevant. I had a packed week trips to manage project programs, client meetings, even a little downtime planned to recharge. But with my foot fucked up, none of that mattered.

I couldn't work, couldn't travel, couldn't even focus on a simple phone call. All my big plans, all my important goals poof, gone. Because when your body s screaming, your brain s got no room for anything else. It wasn't just the physical toll. The mental hit was just as bad.

I'm a guy who thrives on getting shit done writing, speaking, building smart cities. But lying there, I couldn't think straight. My mind was a fog, trapped in this loop of pain and frustration. It made me think about folks who live with chronic pain or illness every day. I used to be quick to judge, to think, Just push through it.

But now? I get it. When pain s your constant companion, it changes you. It humbles you. It makes you see how fragile this whole life thing really is.

What Pain Taught Me So, what did I learn from this shit'show? A few things I'm carrying with me, and I think you should too. 1. Expect the Unexpected Life s a sneaky bastard. You can plan every detail, but one tiny centipede can blow it all to hell.

I had my week mapped out, but that bite forced me to cancel everything. The lesson? Always have a backup plan. Or a backup to your backup. Flexible schedules, understanding colleagues, a little wiggle room in your life these are your lifelines when shit hit's the fan.

Because trust me, it will. 2. Pain s a Brutal Teacher I've always been the type to power through pain, physical or otherwise. But this bite taught me that pain s not just something to ignore s a signal, a red flag waving in your face. It s your body saying, Yo, something s wrong, fix it.

Ignoring it only makes things worse. That pain made me slow down, focus on healing, and appreciate the simple act of walking without wincing. It also made me more careful now I'm checking every corner of my house like it's a crime scene. Pain sucks, but it's got lessons if you listen. 3.

Health Is Everything This is the big one. Health isn't some optional extra you can put off till you less busy. s the foundation of your entire life. Without it, you can't work, can't dream, can't be there for the people you love. Those days in pain showed me that all my achievements, all my hustle, mean jack shit if my body s not on board.

Health is the vehicle that carries you through life. If it breaks down, you're stranded. Zooming Out: Why Health Rules All Let s get philosophical for a sec, because you know I can't help myself. Why is health the ultimate deal? Because it's the only thing that lets you live life on your terms.

When you're healthy, you can chase your goals, laugh with your friends, take care of your mom, or just enjoy a damn good cup of coffee. But when you're not, everything stops. It s like the comedian said if you're healthy, you don't have to worry about anything. If you're not, good luck thinking about anything else. And health isn't just about your body.

It s your mind, your emotions, your spirit. When I was in pain, I couldn't think clearly, couldn't focus on anything but the throbbing in my foot. It was a reminder that mental clarity and emotional stability are just as crucial as physical strength. Stress can make you sick, and sickness can stress you out s a vicious cycle if you don't break it. But here s the good news: we ve got some control over this.

We can eat better, move our bodies, get enough sleep, and listen when our bodies tell us something s off. We can build resilience, not just in our muscles but in our minds, so we're ready for life s curveballs. And we can learn from pain, using it to grow stronger, wiser, more careful. Keep It Simple: Take Care of Yourself So, what's the big takeaway? Prioritize your health.

Make it your number one, non-negotiable, can't-skip-it priority. Because without it, nothing else works. Eat real food, go for a walk, hit the gym, or just take a nap when you need it. And don't ignore the little signals those aches, those twinges. They re trying to tell you something.

Life s unpredictable, and shit will go wrong. But if you take care of your health, you ll be ready to roll with the punches. Like that comedian said, if you're healthy, you ve got nothing to worry about. If you're not, well, you ve got a whole lot on your plate. So, take care of yourself, folks.

Your health is your superpower t let it'slip away.

October 28, 2025

125: Fragments, Chapter 1 Preface This project Fragments is something I never thought I'd do. I'don't read that much fiction, to be honest. But I've always been drawn to stories, especially the kind that explore the human psyche, blur the line between memory and imagination, and ask unsettling questions about what's real and what's not. I've always loved films that do that Her, Eternal Sunshine, Mulholland Drive, The Game, Before Sunrise. This novel sit's somewhere in that'space: psychological, emotional, fragile, and maybe a bit'surreal.

Let me be clear: this isn't a memoir. It s not a true story. It s a composite a narrative stitched together from fragments of past experiences, conversations, thoughts I've carried since I was young, and emotions I've tried to ignore. I took some real things like Hemingway going to sea before writing The Old Man and the Sea and let them mutate, collide, and morph into something else. Writing this has been liberating.

Vulnerable. A way of making sense of things that never quite made sense when they happened. So I won't say more. I'll just say: thanks for reading. I hope it gets under your skin the way writing it got under mine.

And I'd love to know what you think. Non Fragments: Chapter 1 I was there, in a hospital, lying down feeling the pain creeping from my right foot up to my spine. My brain kept telling me that I would be fine. Pain would subside after the brain had adapted to such pain. But that was far from being the case.

I flipped my body around. One minute I was sleeping on left side, the next minute I would be lying down on my back. Then, the process repeated. This wasn't helping me at all in feeling less pain. It just distracted me, numbed my senses, so that I could battle the pain, alone.

My fianc went back an hour ago as the visiting hours we're reached. There I was: Just me, staring at a blank ceiling of a room in an in-patient ward of a small hospital in the suburb of Bangkok. As the pain was getting more excruciating, I was there, with tears pouring down on my cheeks equally from both sides of my eyes. "What did I'do to deserve this?" It must be true that people see things in the moment of pain. Some see themselves in the past.

Some see themselves in the future. I saw both. But the problem is that I saw both through the most pessimistic lens possible. I constantly asked the nurses for more pain-killer shot to go into my blood vein through an IV who'se needle immersed deeply into the skin of my left hand. "We could only give it to you every 3 hours, otherwise it would be dangerous," said a middle-aged nurse who'se face was half covered by a surgical mask.

To be honest, I never felt comfortable with that. Since I was a child, I read lips while listening. It's one of those techniques I had developed as a child and it had helped me to fully understand what the other was saying. The masked nurse walked away. No matter how many times I pushed the intercom because the pain was unbearable, I couldn't seem to convince her to help me with more painkiller.

"How about some morphine?" Of course I just thought to myself. I wouldn't have the courage to ask for such a strong level of anesthetic. Last night, when I first walked myself to this hospital's emergency room yes, the pain had increased over time a young female emergency doctor told me that a centipede's bite like this could be dangerous. The bite wasn't painful. It was quick.

I felt as though I was bitten by something could be an ant. The moment I looked down and saw a one-foot-long centipede on top of my left foot, I screamed and moved my foot recklessly to get it off. I was fast enough to snap a couple of blurred photos of this monster, which I promptly showed to the doctor when asked by protocol what brought me to the emergency room at midnight. The doctor prescribed some painkiller pills for me, while injecting a shot directly to my left foot. "You're good to go home; rest a lot and take the pills when needed," said the young doctor.

At one point, she also casually dropped "The pain shouldn't be a problem to an adult." Believe it or not, that'sentence stuck in my head for a while. I booked a ride home after that. My lovely mother with whom I lived, too, was optimistic that I would be able to cope with the pain by myself. She even told my fianc that I had never experienced anything like that'since I was born, but "as a man" I should be able to tolerate it. Boy, these words from both the young doctor and my mother we're the reason why I'didn't go back to the hospital after the pain got worse.

"I am a man," I told myself while torrents of tears we're pouring out like a broken dam. That night at home was worse than the night at the hospital. Don't get me wrong: They're both abysmally bad, but the first night by myself was something else. In my room alone, I'made noise all night. Any sounds that you might have heard people made when they're in pain I'made all of them.

I cried. I screamed. I told myself that perhaps I should die. I paced in my room, which didn't lessen the pain but at least it made me feel different. Little did I know that doing so would make my foot swollen to almost one-third larger than my other foot.

I would try to lay down whenever the pain allowed. I believed that at least as I was already lying down, somehow I'might be able to fall asleep. I was wrong. For nine hours since I came back from the hospital, I'didn't sleep even a second. Let's rewind a bit here.

The night before I found myself staring blankly at an old cracking ceiling of a hospital listening to the humming of an overly loud noise from an old air-conditioning unit in my single patient room, as you could probably guess by now, I was bitten by a one-foot-long centipede. I was craving food after smoking a joint at the balcony of my room earlier. So, I came down to the first floor with the hope of finding something sweet or savory to satisfy my artificial cannabis-induced hunger. I had lived in this house for more than 4 decades, so I'didn't even bother flipping the light switch when walking to the airfryer at the far corner of my kitchen. That was, as you and I later learned, a fatal mistake.

The only consolation is that there we're many news reports of many people being bitten also by centipedes. This might be due to the rain and humidity that got these crawling creatures to find places to hide. I'didn't think it was necessary to go to the hospital since there we're just small bites on my right foot. I cleaned the foot and poured some medical-grade alcohol directly onto the bite marks. My mother, who heard my scream, came down to the kitchen and asked me to go to the hospital.

I'did so with a bit of skepticism as the bite marks we're quite small. "This is almost midnight and I would rather go to sleep. The pain would go away tomorrow naturally," I told myself. Nevertheless, I went to the emergency room anyway as I knew that would be the only way for my mother to go back to bed. The emergency room was quiet that night.

Hence, I got all the attention from both the doctor and the nurses. They we're all scared when I showed the photos of the centipede that bit me. It took about half an hour for them to clean the wounds and eventually send me home. At home, I started to feel the pain. My mother couldn't sleep as she was waiting for me with my two dogs in the bedroom.

She asked me what the doctor said. After I told her, she smiled and told me that I was old enough to deal with it. I believed her, yet the pain didn't subside as we moved into the quieter part of the night. Yet, there's nothing quiet about it, at least to my mom's and my two dogs' ears. I screamed and cried.

It's like your foot was trapped between a car and it's door and there's someone who kept slamming the door against your foot. You told them to stop, but no one listened. The night was both painful and psychedelic. This was the first time ever that I'didn't have to hide from my mother that I would need to smoke some more joints to get through the night. "Smoke as much as you want this one time," she said.

So, I smoked three more. Since I'didn't have joints left, I'dug up my glass bong from my storage. As I was stuffing the weed into the bong, my hands we're shaking because of the pain. Spoiler alert: Weed didn't make the pain more bearable. It just distracted me from the present moment of pain taking me places as my imagination allowed.

I was traveling to the past. I was peeking into the immediate future. All of these we're helpful in giving me slices of moments of distraction from the task at hand: Stay calm. How could I? I endured the pain until the morning came.

My mother who came to check on me told me I both looked frail and weak. She saw the remnants of the tears on my cheek and, this time, forced me to go to the hospital again. "Admit yourself to the hospital this time," she said. Not gonna lie; I still held a bit of grudge against my beloved mother. "Wasn't it you who told me to man up and suck it up?" The emergency room's doctor in the morning was an older male.

He seemed surprised that I was sent home last night. "These types of venom can be dangerous; they should have admitted you last night." Duh. I ended up spending the entire day and one extra night there. The doctor came to check on me every few hours with nurses who would prepare a shot of painkiller. I couldn't think.

I couldn't do anything. All I could do was hope that the pain would go away. My fianc rushed to the hospital after I sent her a quick text before being moved to the in-patient ward. I would not be looking at my phone again until much later as I couldn't think about doing anything else. "Why me?" I constantly asked myself as if the answer would help.

It didn't. She brought me some snacks and spent the whole day with me until the time she had to go home. A good friend also dropped by with a pack of cannabis gummies. "This would help you, bro." I looked at him with the greatest possible feeling of appreciation. "Thanks, man.

I know for a fact that it wouldn't help with the pain but maybe with something else," I told him. Distractions. Yes, I needed them. For the past few years, I had been feeling empty. Not because I'didn't have anything.

Quite the opposite, it's because I had everything: a house, a car, a respected and stable job, a lovely fianc , and two lovely dogs. All of these we're in addition to the fact that I got to live with my mother taking care of her after my father left us more than a decade ago. "What a perfect life," my good friend once told me. This friend didn't know shit about stability. The gummies took me back to the time when I was just a graduate student abroad trying to learn a new language, making new friends, and trying to survive in a completely different academic environment.

"At least, despite all the hardships, my life was so exciting," I screamed at one point so loud that a nurse would come check on me. "Sorry about that. It's just pain," I brushed off the scream as something to be expected. The nurse looked at a clock in my room and gently told me that'she could give me more painkillers but in an hour, not then. I nodded and went back to my weed-infused imagination.

I enjoyed weed because it helped me to feel distracted from the stability of my life. At that time, I thought to myself that it would be nice if things became stable. But now that I had all the stability I ever needed, I felt as though I was living in a void. I'drank and socialized. I read books.

I traveled. I'did all these because I was under the false hope that they would spice up my life. It didn't. The emptiness continued to creep in. I often wondered to myself what's the purpose of life after all.

Since I was young, I always had vivid dreams. Maybe it's not as great as a lucid one, but I could never tell that I was dreaming. I guess that was common, yet I always asked "What's the difference between our dream and our reality?" I was never shy about sharing with good friends my "simulation theory" that I had believed in ever since I was a boy. Perhaps, just perhaps, we we're here because the real us wanted some distractions from their "stable lives." So, my theory went: In the future, people will live in such an abundant world that they could get whatever they want at their fingertips. Want food?

An automated drone would deliver in a nick of time. Jobs? No one has to do it anymore because humans would have mastered the utilities of robots and artificial intelligence. Governments around the world would send checks to everyone because it's cheaper and the most effective way to maintain a "stable society." No jobs? Don't worry.

Here's the check just use it and don't cause any trouble. Some call it a universal basic income. In that future, people would get so bored that they would ask to be put in a simulation just to feel "distracted" from the monotony of life. These could just be all weed talking. I was in pain and the gummies contained 50 mg of the substance that made you high.

Usually, 5 mg could already kill those who didn't use it regularly. I was taking ten times of that. The same pattern of pain as the night before emerged but this time I couldn't pace anymore. I could only lie down on the bed and scream, sometimes inside and sometimes, needless to say, out loud. The night went by slowly.

I lost a few kilos of weight just by the lack of appetite, and the pain. But, but, there's one surprising thing that came out of this stoned cold long night: I actually enjoyed some aspects of pain. I could have come to the hospital again after the venom made me want to jump off my balcony last night, but I'didn't. Why? Well, because in the pain, there we're parts of it that I enjoyed.

Although I prayed that this was all just a bad dream and then when I woke up everything would be fine, a part of me wanted to feel it deeply. My mind split: A version of me kept saying "This is all just a simulation" while the other part was preaching to me "Pain made you feel alive, isn't it?" My fianc came to visit me in the morning. The pain did subside. I felt frail and weak. We had a light hospital lunch together on my bed before driving me home.

I spent the entire afternoon in bed at home feeling weak and tired. "I wish the pain would last a bit longer it did make me feel alive." While still high from the gummy and all the weed I had smoked the night before, my mind wandered. It traveled as far as 2 millennia BC and around the same number of years forward. But there was one particular moment that kept replaying again and again in my head because it gave me a similar feeling though without the physical pain. That moment got me thinking about everything from whether or not I could and should trust my intuition, to whether or not any of the things we we're seeing even real.

No, it's not like another version of a simulation theory, but it's more as though whether or not we we're slaves to something else darker, more sinister, and more insidious. Thinking about that moment made me feel as though there we're, not one, but a dozen of centipedes crawling all over me: wrapping around my torso, hiking up and down my spine, and crisscrossing with it's many claws on my face. It's the moment that made me question everything: Am I real? Am I human? Am I seeing things from a parallel universe?

That moment began a couple of weeks earlier. When I was in Kuala Lumpur.
